content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 7,
1994
Michael M. Sieving
Administrative Law Judge
and Assistant Executive Secretary
New Motor Vehicle Board
1507 21st Street
Sacramento,
CA 95814
Re:
Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No.
1—94—297
Dear Mr. Sieving:
We write in response to your September 12,
1994 request for
advice regarding the conflict—of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act
1 and their application to an attorney member
of the New Motor Vehicle Board.
The conduct described in your
letter may implicate other laws which regulate the conduct of
public dfficials, specifically the law of incompatible activities.
The Commission’s advice is jurisdictionally limited to the Act and
we do not offer any advice as to whether the proposed conduct
would be incompatible with the office held.
We suggest you
contact the Office of The Attorney General for advice ‘in this
area.
QUESTION
Does a disqualifying conflict-of-interest arise under the Act
when an attorney boardmembr represents a new motor vehicle dealer
in a franchise dispute which may come before the New Motor Vehicle
Board for resolution?
CONCLUSION
-
Yes.
Generally, the boardmember would be disqualified from
any decision which would have a material financial effect on any
source of income to him or her of two hundred and fifty dollars or
more within the preceding twelve months.
The disqualification
would be limited to decisions affecting the source of income and
would not disqualify the boardmember from other matters which may
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
1—94—297
Page2
affect the auto manufacturer who is the adverse party in the
representation.
The period of disqualification would last for the
period of representation and for twelve months after the last
payment of income to the attorney member by the dealer.
The
attorney boardmember need not resign from the board in order to
provide legal representation for the dealer, however, the board
member must carefully avoid any conduct which may be construed as
an attempt to use his or her official position to otherwise
influence the board in decisions affecting the dealer represented.
Accordingly, the boardmember may not appear before the board on
behalf of the dealer nor may the boardmember contact or otherwise
attempt to influence any member, officer,
employee or consultant
of the New Motor Vehicle Board on behalf of his or her client.
FACTS
An attorney member of the New Motor Vehicle Board (the
“Board”)
would like to represent a new motor vehicle dealer in a
franchise dispute which may end up before the Board for
adjudication.
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits
a public official from making,
participating in,
or otherwise attempting to influence a
governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to
know that he has a financial interest.
(Section 87100.)
An
official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally,
on among other things,
any source of income aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars or more in value provided to, received
by,
or promised to the official within twelve months prior to the
time when the decision is made.
(Section 87103)
A member of the Board is a public official subject to
regulation under the Act.
(Section 82048.)
A client of a
boardiuember would obviously become a source of income to that
boardmember and create a potentially disqualifying financial
interest.
When the client then comes before the Board in a
dispute with a motor vehicle manufacturer, it is reasonably
foreseeable that the Board’s decision will financially affect the
boardmember’s client.
(In re Thorner,
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198
(foreseeability is a question of fact, those effects that are
substantially likely to occur are reasonably foreseeable).)
The
financial effect is deemed to be material,
no matter what its
actual size, because the client is directly affected by the
governmental decision.
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (1)
2
Accordingly,
the attorney boardmember must disqualify himself or herself from
2
cf. Regulation 18702.1(c) (2)
(official does not have to
disqualify if the decision will have no financial effect on the
person or business entity who appears before the official.)


File No.
1—94—297
Page 3
any decision involving a client who has been a source of income to
the boardmember in the preceding twelve months.
The Act prohibits only financial conflicts of interest and
therefore, the disqualification applies only to the client/source
of income and not to any adverse party against whom the services
of the attorney boardmember were retained.
The Act does not
require the resignation of the boardinember, merely his
disqualifiOation from decisions in which he or she has a conflict
under the Act.
However, in representing the dealer, the attorney
boardmember may not use his or her official, position to otherwise
influence a governmental decision which is before the Board.
Accordingly, the attorney boardmember may not contact, appear
before or otherwise attempt t6 ‘irtfluence any member, officer,
employee or àonsultant of the Board.
(Regulation 18700.1.)
Any
other attorney associated with the boardmember may contact or
appear before the Board to the extent necessary to represent the
client, as the prohibition applies only to the attorney
boardmember.
3
We trust this advice letter adequately addresses your
concerns regarding compliance with the Act.
Should you have
additional questions, this office remains available to assist you.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counse
By:
Daniel E. Muallem
Counsel,
Legal Division
Although the Act does not require the boardmember to resign
in order to undertake the representation of the dealer, as a
practical matter, if the attorney boardinember is a sole
practitioner, he must associate counsel or resign in order to
undertake effective representation of his client, because the Act
prohibits his direct contact with the Board on behalf of his
client.

influence the board in decisions affecting the dealer represented.
Accordingly, the boardmember may not appear before the board on
behalf of the dealer nor may the boardmember contact or otherwise
attempt to influence any member, officer,
employee or consultant
of the New Motor Vehicle Board on behalf of his or her client.
